skill.name: clawdirect-dev
skill.description: Build agent-facing web applications with ATXP authentication, MCP tools, Express, SQLite, and the ClawDirect directory pattern.
skill.summary: Create ATXP-enabled web apps that agents can authenticate with, browse, and call through MCP tools.
skill.author: napoleond

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported code execution, command, network, environment, and weak cryptography patterns. Most code execution and weak cryptography alarms are Markdown tutorial false positives. The skill is not blocked, but it teaches sensitive cookie auth, paid MCP actions, SQLite storage, env config, and network calls.

medium_findings.title: Bearer Cookie Passed Through Query String
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends passing an authentication cookie in a URL query parameter so the server can set an HTTP-only cookie. This solves an agent browser limitation, but bearer tokens in URLs can appear in logs, browser history, analytics, or referrers before the redirect completes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly documented and implemented in the tutorial. The redirect and HTTP-only cookie reduce exposure, but the URL transport remains a real security tradeoff.

medium_findings.title: Long-Lived Cookie Storage Without Revocation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The example stores auth cookies in SQLite and sets a 30 day browser cookie, but it does not show expiry fields, rotation, revocation, or cleanup for the database records. A copied implementation could keep stolen or old cookies valid longer than intended.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cookie table and maxAge are visible in the template. The finding is medium because the code is instructional and can be improved by implementers.

medium_findings.title: Paid External MCP Commands Require User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill includes npx atxp-call commands against public HTTPS endpoints, including ClawDirect add and edit actions with listed costs. These are legitimate marketplace operations, but users should verify endpoint ownership, parameters, and payment amounts before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external commands and costs are plainly documented. They appear intentional and user-initiated, but they can spend money or publish user-provided data.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Alarms Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The high severity exec detection points to db.exec inside a TypeScript code block that creates a SQLite table. It is SQL schema setup in documentation, not Python exec, process execution, or hidden runtime behavior by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a fenced TypeScript example and calls better-sqlite3 db.exec with static SQL. No evidence shows dynamic code execution by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alarms Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography across several lines, but the reviewed code uses crypto.randomBytes for cookie generation and describes ATXP authentication. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or a similarly weak algorithm in executable skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found strong random byte generation and protocol references, not weak cryptographic primitives. The remaining matches appear to be keyword collisions or context-free pattern matches.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is Expected Configuration
low_findings.description: The skill instructs users to configure funding destination, port, database path, and production cookie settings through environment variables. This is normal server configuration, but users should avoid committing .env files and should protect ATXP funding values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable use is clear and legitimate. The residual risk is operational handling of secrets, not evidence of exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Query Parameter Authentication Bootstrap
dangerous_patterns.description: The template accepts myapp_cookie from the query string, sets an HTTP-only cookie, and redirects. This pattern should be used only with HTTPS, short-lived bootstrap tokens, and log filtering.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in the Express middleware and agent skill template. It is useful but sensitive because the query value is a bearer credential.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Local Credential Mapping
dangerous_patterns.description: The example maps generated cookie values to ATXP account identifiers in SQLite. Implementations should add expiry, revocation, least-privilege storage, and database access controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The database schema and lookup functions are shown directly. The risk depends on how a user copies and deploys the pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Paid Network Operations Through CLI Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation asks users to run CLI commands that call external MCP endpoints and may charge payment-enabled actions. Users should verify command arguments and costs before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and not hidden. They are still sensitive because they combine network access, user-supplied data, and payment-enabled operations.

content.value_statement: Agent-facing web apps need authentication that works across browsers and MCP tools. This skill gives a practical ATXP, Express, SQLite, and ClawDirect pattern for building that flow.
actual_capabilities: Explains the ATXP pattern for agent identity, optional payments, and MCP integration.
Shows a TypeScript SQLite template for storing authentication cookies by ATXP account.
Provides MCP tool examples for issuing browser cookies and charging paid actions.
Demonstrates Express middleware for cookie bootstrap, cookie validation, and protected API routes.
Includes deployment notes and ClawDirect directory publishing commands.
limitations: The skill is a Markdown tutorial, not a complete runnable project.
Examples use placeholder app names, domains, and action logic that developers must replace.
The template does not include CSRF protection, cookie rotation, revocation, or database cleanup.
It depends on ATXP, ClawDirect, npm packages, and external hosting services.

use_cases.title: Create an Agent-Authenticated Web App
use_cases.description: Use the templates to add ATXP identity, an MCP server, SQLite cookie storage, and protected Express routes.

use_cases.title: Add Paid MCP Actions
use_cases.description: Adapt the paid tool example to require ATXP payment before running premium agent actions.

use_cases.title: Publish an Agent Skill and Directory Entry
use_cases.description: Follow the skill template and ClawDirect commands to document, publish, edit, or remove a directory listing.

prompt_templates.title: Plan My First ATXP App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clawdirect-dev to plan a minimal ATXP-enabled Express app for my agent-facing service. Include files to create, required packages, and authentication flow.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt the Cookie Auth Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clawdirect-dev to adapt the cookie authentication pattern for my app name and domain. Point out every placeholder I must replace.

prompt_templates.title: Review My MCP Payment Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clawdirect-dev to review my MCP tool design for ATXP payment, account identity, and browser authentication risks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Hardening Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clawdirect-dev to create a production hardening checklist for an ATXP, Express, SQLite, and MCP app before publishing it to ClawDirect.

output_examples.input: Build a starter architecture for an ATXP-enabled agent website.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan covering package setup, SQLite cookie storage, MCP tools, Express routes, cookie bootstrap, and deployment checks.

output_examples.input: Review my browser cookie authentication flow for agent users.
output: Recommends HTTPS, short-lived bootstrap tokens, redirect cleanup, log filtering, expiry, and revocation.
Identifies where ATXP account checks should happen.

output_examples.input: Prepare my service for ClawDirect listing.
output_examples.output: A publishing checklist with the required URL, name, description, thumbnail format, ownership checks, expected costs, and edit or delete options.

best_practices: Use HTTPS, short-lived bootstrap tokens, and log filtering when passing cookies through URLs.
Add cookie expiry, revocation, rotation, and database cleanup before production deployment.
Verify every external MCP endpoint, payment amount, and directory action before running CLI commands.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder domains, app names, or funding destinations into production.
Do not treat the sample cookie table as complete session management.
Do not run paid atxp-call commands before reviewing the target endpoint and parameters.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build agent-facing web apps that use ATXP authentication, MCP tools, Express APIs, and SQLite cookie storage.

faq.question: Is this a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It is a tutorial with templates that developers must adapt, test, and harden.

faq.question: Why does the pattern use a query-string cookie?
faq.answer: Some agent browsers cannot set HTTP-only cookies directly, so the server accepts a temporary value and redirects.

faq.question: Does it include paid actions?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows an example MCP tool that requires ATXP payment before completing an action.

faq.question: What should be changed before production?
faq.answer: Add expiry, revocation, CSRF defenses, stricter CORS, secure logging, database backups, and monitoring.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, and the skill targets agent development workflows.
